config.title: Data Structures
config.description: Arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs, and their time complexities.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of accessing an element by index in an array?
questions.hint: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory — any index is directly reachable.
questions.explanation: Arrays provide constant-time random access because elements are stored contiguously. The CPU computes base_address + (index × element_size) in one operation.

questions.question: In a singly linked list, each node contains:

options.label: A
options.text: Data and a pointer to the previous node

options.label: B
options.text: Data and a pointer to the next node

options.label: C
options.text: Data, a pointer to the next node, and a pointer to the previous node

questions.explanation: Each node in a singly linked list holds its data value and one pointer to the next node. A doubly linked list adds a pointer to the previous node.

questions.question: Inserting an element at the beginning of an array is O(1).
questions.hint: What happens to every other element when you insert at index 0?
questions.explanation: Inserting at the beginning of an array is O(n) because every existing element must shift one position to the right to make room.

questions.question: A stack follows which ordering principle?
questions.hint: Think of a stack of plates.
questions.explanation: A stack is LIFO — the last element pushed is the first to be popped. Common analogy: a stack of plates where you can only add or remove from the top.

questions.question: Which data structure is used to implement breadth-first search (BFS)?
questions.hint: BFS explores level by level — what does 'first come, first explored' suggest?
questions.explanation: BFS uses a queue (FIFO) to process nodes level by level. Each discovered neighbor is enqueued; the oldest node is always dequeued and processed next.

questions.question: A queue supports O(1) enqueue and O(1) dequeue when implemented with a doubly linked list.
questions.hint: Enqueue adds to one end, dequeue removes from the other — both O(1) with a list.
questions.explanation: With a doubly linked list, enqueue appends to the tail and dequeue removes from the head, both in O(1) time. An array-based queue requires shifting or using a circular buffer.

questions.question: In a binary search tree (BST), for any node N:

options.label: A
options.text: All nodes in the left subtree are greater than N

options.label: B
options.text: All nodes in the left subtree are less than N

options.label: C
options.text: Left and right subtrees have equal depth

options.label: D
options.text: N is always the smallest element

questions.hint: Values increase as you go right.
questions.explanation: BST invariant: left child < parent < right child. For every node N, all values in its left subtree are smaller, and all values in its right subtree are larger.

questions.question: What is the height of a balanced binary tree with n nodes?
questions.hint: Each level roughly doubles the number of nodes.
questions.explanation: A balanced binary tree with n nodes has height O(log n). Each level doubles the node count, so we need about log₂(n) levels to fit n nodes.

questions.question: An in-order traversal of a valid BST always yields elements in sorted ascending order.
questions.hint: In-order = Left → Node → Right. Combine with BST property.
questions.explanation: In-order traversal visits left subtree → root → right subtree recursively. Given the BST invariant (left < root < right), this always produces values in sorted ascending order.

questions.question: Which graph representation has O(1) edge lookup but O(V²) space complexity?
questions.hint: It's a 2D grid of V × V.
questions.explanation: An adjacency matrix stores a V×V boolean grid. Checking if edge (u,v) exists is O(1), but the space cost is O(V²), making it wasteful for sparse graphs.

questions.question: Depth-first search (DFS) is typically implemented using:
questions.hint: DFS goes deep before wide — what tracks where to backtrack?
questions.explanation: DFS uses a stack — explicitly or via the call stack in recursive implementations. It pushes unvisited neighbors and backtracks by popping, exploring one branch fully before another.

questions.question: A tree is a special case of a graph.
questions.hint: What defines a graph? Vertices and edges — does a tree have those?
questions.explanation: A tree is an acyclic, connected undirected graph with exactly V-1 edges for V nodes. Every tree is a graph, but not every graph is a tree.

questions.question: What is the time complexity of search in an unbalanced BST in the worst case?
questions.hint: Imagine inserting values 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 in order into a BST.
questions.explanation: Inserting already-sorted data into a BST degenerates it into a linked list. Search then requires traversing all n nodes in the worst case, giving O(n).

questions.question: What is the amortized time complexity of appending to a dynamic array (e.g. Python list)?
questions.hint: The array doubles in size occasionally, but how often does that happen?
questions.explanation: Dynamic arrays double capacity when full. Although individual resizes cost O(n), they happen infrequently enough that the cost is amortized across all appends, giving O(1) amortized time.

questions.question: Dijkstra's algorithm fails on graphs with:
questions.hint: The greedy shortest-path assumption breaks down when a path can decrease.
questions.explanation: Dijkstra's greedy approach assumes that once a node is finalized, no shorter path exists. Negative edges can create shorter paths discovered later, violating this assumption. Use Bellman-Ford instead.

questions.question: A priority queue retrieves elements in which order?
questions.hint: It's like a queue, but with VIP access.
questions.explanation: A priority queue always dequeues the element with the highest (or lowest, depending on implementation) priority, regardless of insertion order. Typically implemented with a heap for O(log n) operations.

questions.question: A min-heap guarantees that the smallest element is always at the root.
questions.hint: Think of the heap property: parent ≤ children.
questions.explanation: In a min-heap, the heap property requires every parent to be less than or equal to its children. This means the minimum element is always at the root, accessible in O(1).

questions.question: Which operation is O(n) in a singly linked list but O(1) in a doubly linked list (given a pointer to the node)?

options.label: B
options.text: Deleting a node given its pointer

questions.hint: To delete in a singly linked list, you need the previous node — how do you find it?
questions.explanation: Deleting a node in a singly linked list requires finding the predecessor to update its next pointer, which costs O(n). In a doubly linked list, the previous pointer is directly accessible, enabling O(1) deletion.

flashcards.back: A LIFO data structure where the last element inserted is the first removed.
For every node N: all values in the left subtree < N < all values in the right subtree.
Matrix: O(1) edge lookup, O(V²) space. List: O(degree) edge lookup, O(V+E) space.
Dynamic arrays double capacity on overflow. Rare O(n) resizes are spread across n appends, giving O(1) amortized cost.
Every parent node is less than or equal to its children. The minimum is always at the root.

terminology.LIFO: Last In, First Out — the ordering principle of a stack.
terminology.FIFO: First In, First Out — the ordering principle of a queue.
terminology.BST: Binary Search Tree — a binary tree where left < node < right.
terminology.Heap: A complete binary tree satisfying the heap property (min or max at root).
terminology.Adjacency Matrix: A V×V matrix representing graph edges; O(1) lookup, O(V²) space.
terminology.DFS: Depth-First Search — explores as far as possible before backtracking.
terminology.BFS: Breadth-First Search — explores all neighbors before going deeper.

achievements.title: On A Roll
achievements.description: Achieve a streak of 5 correct answers.
